coredata.dc:title: Spore-derived color-tunable multi-doped carbon nanodots as sensitive nanosensors and intracellular imaging agents
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yabin
Chan, Kai Fung
Wang, Ben
Chiu, Philip Wai Yan
Zhang, Li
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots hold great promise in biomedical real-time tracking due to their outstanding optical properties and biocompatibility. Here, we report a one-pot synthetic strategy to produce multi-doped carbon dots (MCDs) with bright and color-tunable emission. The resultant MCDs are doped by abundant biogenic elements (O, N, P), so that they contain a variety of surface functional groups. Such hetero-doping and functionalization make MCDs display strong fluorescent emission and excitation-wavelength-dependent characteristic, good aqueous solubility as well as high photo-stability and ion-stability. Moreover, the as-prepared MCDs response to the change of pH values and exhibit high selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions under blue light illumination with the limit of detection (LOD) of 15.9 nM. Such a Fe3+ sensing ability is further demonstrated in real water samples and extended to intracellular multi-color monitoring of Fe3+. More interestingly, the MCDs can also sense the gastrointestinal bacteria Escherichia coli, exhibiting linear sensing dependent behavior. With low cytotoxicity and tunable emission, such MCDs hold great potential as efficient multi-color probes and imaging agents for clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multi-doped carbon dots
Fe3+ intracellular detection
Multicolor cellular imaging
E. coli sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Response to pH and various ions and molecules
Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ions and bacteria
Fluorescent response of MCDs towards Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The flow chart of the preparation of MCDs and the potential sensing and imaging application. Collected spores sprayed by Ganoderma lucidum at maturity stage were treated hydrothermally to obtain fluorescent carbon dots, which not only serve as sensing platforms for iron ions and bacteria, but also act as multicolor imaging agents.
(A-C) TEM images of MCDs with low- and high-magnification. (D) Size distribution histogram of MCDs. (E) FTIR spectra and XPS survey spectra (F) of spore and spore-derived MCDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescent spectra of the MCDs (excitation at 360 nm and emission at 440 nm). Inset shows the optical images of the MCDs solution under white light and UV light illumination. (B) Fluorescent emissions under different excitation wavelength. (C) The changing curve of fluorescent intensity with diverse pH values under UV light illumination, inset shows the corresponding photos under white light and UV light. (D) The changing curve of fluorescent intensity with diverse pH values under blue light illumination.

The corresponding blue fluorescent response (F/F0) of the MCDs based nanosensors in aqueous solution of (A) cations and (B) anions and molecules in the absence (black column) and presence (red column) of Fe3+. Photographs of the MCDs solution with the addition of cations (C), anions and molecules (D) under 360 nm UV lamp irradiation. (E) Fluorescent emission spectra of the MCDs at 440 nm after adding different concentrations of Fe3+ from 0 to 500 μM. (F) Relationship between the fluorescent quenching efficiency (F0/F-1) and the concentration of Fe3+. Inset shows the corresponding optical images under UV light irradiation. Fitting linear relationship between (F0/F-1) and the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 2.5–100 nM under the excitation of 360 nm (G) and 460 nm light (H). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Schematic illustration of the plausible fluorescence detecting mechanism of the MCDs towards Fe3+ions. (B) Fluorescent decay curves of the MCDs in the absence or presence of Fe3+ with different concentration (Ex = 360 nm, Em = 450 nm).
Fluorescence images of MIHA cells using 0.50 mg/mL MCDs and MCDs + 500 μM Fe3+ excited by 405 nm, 488 nm, and 559 nm. Scale bar is 30 μm.

head.ce:title: Spore-derived color-tunable multi-doped carbon nanodots as sensitive nanosensors and intracellular imaging agents

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Automation Engineering, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shatin NT, Hong Kong SAR, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Automation Engineering
The Chinese University of Hong Kong

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shatin NT, Hong Kong SAR, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chow Yuk Ho Technology Centre for Innovative Medicine, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shatin NT, Hong Kong SAR, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chow Yuk Ho Technology Centre for Innovative Medicine
The Chinese University of Hong Kong

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Surgery, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shatin NT, Hong Kong SAR, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Mechanical and Automation Engineering, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shatin NT, Hong Kong SAR, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Automation Engineering
The Chinese University of Hong Kong

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multi-doped carbon dots were synthesized via a one-pot synthetic strategy without any further surface passivation. The as-obtained MCDs can selectively and sensitively detect Fe3+ and E. coli bacteria, even and measuring the intracellular Fe3+ through multi-color fluorescence imaging.

Fluorescent carbon dots hold great promise in biomedical real-time tracking due to their outstanding optical properties and biocompatibility. Here, we report a one-pot synthetic strategy to produce multi-doped carbon dots (MCDs) with bright and color-tunable emission. The resultant MCDs are doped by abundant biogenic elements (O, N, P), so that they contain a variety of surface functional groups. Such hetero-doping and functionalization make MCDs display strong fluorescent emission and excitation-wavelength-dependent characteristic, good aqueous solubility as well as high photo-stability and ion-stability. Moreover, the as-prepared MCDs response to the change of pH values and exhibit high selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions under blue light illumination with the limit of detection (LOD) of 15.9 nM. Such a Fe3+ sensing ability is further demonstrated in real water samples and extended to intracellular multi-color monitoring of Fe3+. More interestingly, the MCDs can also sense the gastrointestinal bacteria Escherichia coli, exhibiting linear sensing dependent behavior. With low cytotoxicity and tunable emission, such MCDs hold great potential as efficient multi-color probes and imaging agents for clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Anthropological activities make us have easy access to a variety of metal ions, molecular compounds and bacteria, which may be accumulated in human body through food chain. For example, iron overload or deficiency can disturb cellular homeostasis and metabolism, further resulting in various diseases, such as hepatitis, cancer, neurodegeneration Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease, and so on [1,2]. The increasing multidrug-resistant bacteria around us pose a serious threat to public health [3]. Thus, it is vital to selectively and sensitively monitor them in environmental components, agri-food and even biological samples. Conventional analytic strategies either require high-end instrumentation, sophisticated operation, or complex sample preparation steps and expensive probes [4,5]. The emergence of fluorescence-based technique provides a new solution to ions detection owing to its high sensitivity, low cost, great simplicity, non-destructing to specimen, and capability of real-time monitoring. Numerous fluorescent probes including organic dyes [6], fluorescent metal nanoclusters [7], semiconductor quantum dots [8], and fluorescent metal organic frameworks [9] have been developed in the past years. However, their practical applications are still restricted by their cytotoxicity, poor solubility in aqueous media, environmental unfriendliness, high cost, and tedious treatment processes.

Fluorescent carbon dots have attracted tremendous interest over organic fluorophores and semiconductor quantum dots due to their outstanding optical properties, customizable surface functionalization, excellent biocompatibility, robust chemical inertness, simple and inexpensive preparation, and abundance of raw material in nature. It has been demonstrated that small size, functionalized surface and even hetero-atoms doping make carbon dots possess more active sites or multiple electronic states (i.e., either electron donors or electron acceptors), and easily impacted by external minute perturbation, resulting in the variation of fluorescent intensity. Such an intensity variation can be employed to detect the external influencing substance. Thus, it is imperative to incorporate hetero-atoms into carbon dots to create more defect sites for producing new and unanticipated properties. Until now, a great variety of doped carbon dots were synthesized for emerging chemo- and bio-analytical science [10–12]. Nevertheless, most of successful approaches for color-tunable multi-doped carbon dots mainly utilize various organic molecules as sources, especially amine compounds with toxicity. It is still challenging to achieve the multi-doping of carbon dots with multicolor emission and low cytotoxicity for intracellular ion detection via more cost-effective and facile preparation procedures. Moreover, the detection tests are usually characterized under ultraviolet (UV) excitation, which is harmful to the biological samples and bio-living systems due to the UV excitation risk.

In this work, MCDs were prepared via a facile synthetic strategy, in which natural plant spores were treated hydrothermally without any further surface passivation procedures. The resultant MCDs have an average diameter of 3.1 nm, with abundant functional groups on surfaces, suggesting the inheritance of partial functional groups during the process of synthesis. These structural and interfacial features endow MCDs with strong fluorescent emission and excitation-wavelength-dependent characteristic, good aqueous solubility, photo-stability as well as ion stability. The as-prepared MCDs can not only response to the change of pH values, but also be utilized directly as nanosensors for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions under different light illumination. Such a detecting ability can be extended to rain or tap water and living cell. Low cytotoxic MCDs are also good multi-color imaging agents for intracellular fluorescence.

Ganoderma lucidum spores, seeds of a fungus named “Lingzhi” in China, were purchased from cultured farm in Changbai Mountain, Jilin Province, China. All the reagents were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used as received without further purification. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) and brain heart infusion (BHI) were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Incorporation. Escherichia coli (E. coli), Bacillus subtilis (B.substilis) and agar culture medium (aqueous solution of agar and soy powder) were provided by Department of Biomedical Engineering (BME, The Chinese University of Hong Kong). Deionized water with the resistance of 18.2 MΩ (Smart2pure 12, Thermo scientific) was used throughout the experiments.

Multi-doped carbon dots were obtained by a simple hydrothermal treatment using Ganoderma lucidum spores as the carbon sources. Typically, spores (0.1–2 g) were dispersed into 40 mL deionized water and stirred for 20 min. Then the brown solution was transferred into 45 mL Teflon-line autoclave and heated at different temperature (120 °C–200 °C) for 24 h. After cooled to room temperature, the light brown suspensions of carbon dots were obtained after separating the indispensable matters by vacuum filtration (0.22 μm nitrocellulose filters). Such suspensions were further centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min to remove the non-fluorescent precipitates. The as-obtained suspensions were dialyzed with dialysis membrane (MWCO 3500) for 24 h and then stored at 4 °C for further characterization and applications. Meanwhile, the suspension could be further freeze dried for later use.

The microstructures and sizes of MCDs were taken by a Tecnai F20 transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI, USA) and multimode scanning probe microscopy (NanoScope IVa, Digital Instruments). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Nicolet NEXUS 670 FTIR spectrometer with KBr pellets. UV–vis absorption was performed on an U2910 double beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The composition of samples was further analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi). Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a 1 cm quartz cuvette at room temperature. The excitation and emission slit widths were both set at 5 nm. The fluorescence decay curves of the product were determined by a FLS980 transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh instruments, UK). Zeta potentials of the MCDs in different pH solutions were measured by a Zeta-sizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern, UK). Cellular imaging was carried out on a confocal laser scanning microscopy under the excitation of light with 405 nm, 488 nm and 559 nm (CLSM, Olympus FV1200 SIM Confocal System with ZDC, Japan). Cytotoxicity evaluation was tested based on the MTT result from a microplate reader (Multiskan Mk3, Thermofisher, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response to pH and various ions and molecules
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, NaCl, CH3COONa (NaAc), Na2CO3, Na2SO4, NaNO3, NaOH, KCl, ZnCl2, LiCl, MnAc2·4H2O, NH4Ac, CaCl2·2H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, SnCl2, Mg(NO3)2·2H2O, La(NO3)3·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, SrAc2, CdCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Hg(NO3)2·2H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, AlCl3·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, AgNO3, aspartic acid (Asp), glucose (GLU), melamine (MA), dicyandiamide (DCD), sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (Rh12PhSO3Na), sodium citrate dihydrate (C5H7O5COONa3·2H2O), sodium salicylate (C6H4(OH)COONa), sodium oleate (C17H33COONa) and p-toluenesulfonic acid sodium salt (p-CH3C6H4SO3Na) were added into NaAc-HAc buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 6), respectively. Then 333 μL stock solution of MCDs (3 mg/mL) was dropped sequentially into the above solutions, diluting all the solutions to 2 mL with NaAc-HAc buffer solution to ensure the final concentration of MCDs (0.5 mg/mL) and additives (0.5 mM). The resultant mixtures were ultrasonicated for 5 min. Then the fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were investigated under the optimum conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ions and bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: A stock solution of Fe3+ (1 mM) was prepared in NaAc-HAc buffer solution (pH = 6, 10 mM). Such stock solutions with different volume were mixed thoroughly with 333 μL of stock solution of MCDs. The mixtures were diluted to 2 mL to obtain the required concentrations of Fe3+ (0.001–500 μM) with NaAc-HAc buffer solution. The final solutions with different concentration of Fe3+ were ultrasonicated for 5 min at room temperature, and the fluorescence spectra were then recorded. Meanwhile, the interfering effects of other ions and molecules on Fe3+ detecting ability were also investigated by adding 500 μM Fe3+ solution into the above-prepared ion and molecule solution of 0.5 mM. To verify Fe3+ detection ability of the as-prepared MCDs as nanosensors in real samples, the as-collected environmental samples, rainwater and laboratory tap, were utilized to replace deionized water. Prior to the test, these environmental water samples were filtered via vacuum filtration with a 0.22 μm membrane. Next, 0.5 mL of the sample solutions were sequentially spiked with standard Fe3+ solutions (final concentration in the range of 2.5–100 nM). The spiked samples were then mixed with aliquot of MCDs solution (final concentration 0.5 mg/mL). The above mixture solutions were eventually diluted to 2 mL with NaAc-HAc (10 mM, pH = 6.0) for fluorescent characterization.

E. coli (at a level of 2.148 × 108 cfu/mL) and B. substilis (at a level of 0.8 × 108 cfu/mL), were washed with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.2-7.4) for twice to remove the broth and medium. Then they were resuspended in 3 mL of PBS buffer to obtain the stock solution (at a level of 1.75 × 108 cfu/mL). Next, the above suspensions with different volume were mixed with the as-prepared MCDs solution (0.5 mg/mL). The resultant mixtures were diluted into 2 mL by PBS buffer to fix the final concentration and then incubated at 37 °C for 30 min for fluorescent evaluation.

The standard MTT assays were conducted to evaluate the cytotoxicity of MCDs. MIHA cells (normal liver cells) were seeded into a 96-well plate at a density of 3500 cells per well in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) with 10% FBS and antibiotics (penicillin-streptomycin 100U/mL) and incubated in a humidified incubator containing 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h. Afterward, 90 μL of fresh DMEM containing different doses of MCDs (0, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400, 600 μg/mL) were added into each well to substitute for the culture medium and incubated for another 24 h. Then, 10 uL of MTT solution (stock solution 5 mg/mL in PBS) was added to each well, and incubated for 4 h. After removing the culture medium with MTT, DMSO was added into each well and followed by 15 min shaking to dissolve the formed formazan crystals. The absorbance of each well at 570 nm was recorded by microplate reader with pure DMSO as a blank. A non-treated cell was also used as a control and the relative cell viability was expressed as Abssample/Abscontrol × 100%.

MIHA cells were seeded on 35 mm cultured dishes with the above media and incubated in a humidified incubator containing 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, fresh culture media with 0.5 mg/mL MCDs were added to each well for another 24 h of incubation. Then, MIHA cells treated above in three cultured dishes were further incubated with 500 μM Fe3+ at 37 °C for 6 h. Subsequently, all the cells were washed with PBS several times (>3 times). Finally, the MCD labeled samples were examined by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) with a fixed excitation wavelength of 405, 488, and 559 nm.

The synthetic process of MCDs only involved simple hydrothermal treatment of artificial cultured Ganoderma lucidum spore powders without other complex operations. Spore, as a seed of plant to proliferate the next generation, contains plenty of nutrient components including amide, triterpene, polysaccharide, sterols, fatty acid, lipid, terpene, proteins, and a typical feature of sugars. The carbohydrates among them can dehydrate, polymerizate, and carbonize during the hydrothermal process, accompanied by the incorporation of functional molecules, eventually forming the doped carbon dots [13]. As shown in Fig. 1 , the non-emissive brown spore solution can be converted directly into canary yellow solution with blue emission under UV light illumination. The synthesized MCDs can serve as a sensing platform for Fe3+ ion as well as multicolor imaging agents. The fabrication protocol of highly and multiply hetero-doped carbon dots avoids the usage of organic toxic chemicals such as amine compounds in a single step and thus conforms to the requirements of green chemistry. A series of MCDs prepared at different reaction conditions were conducted to optimize the reaction condition. After evaluating the emission spectra as illustrated in Fig. S1, the final MCDs were prepared at 180 °C for 24 h using 1 g of natural spores as sources. The obtained MCDs possess high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of around 14.5% at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm by using quinine hemisulfate (QS) monohydrate as the reference, which is significantly higher than the previously reported carbon dots treated by surface passivation [14–16].

The resultant spore-derived MCDs show the quasi-spherical morphology and good mono-dispersity (well separated from each other in Fig. 2 A). The corresponding size distribution histogram from one hundred particles displays that such MCDs have diameters ranging from 1 to 6 nm with the statistic diameter of 3.1 ± 0.4 nm (Fig. 2D), which is further supported by atomic force microscopy imaging in Fig. S2. The high resolution TEM images (Fig. 2 B and C) show a well-resolved lattice structure with a d-spacing value of 0.24 nm, which may be attributed to the (1120) diffraction planes of graphitic carbon. This confirms the crystalline graphite-like structure of the as-prepared MCDs [17,18].

As shown in FTIR spectra in Fig. 2E, the obtained MCDs greatly inherit many functional groups from the original spores. The broad band from 3100 to 3600 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of NH and OH [19]. The absorption peaks at approximately 2300, 1745 and 1643 cm−1 can be attributed to the CN stretching, CC/CN stretching and CO species, respectively [20]. The peaks appearing at around 1542 cm−1 and 1254 cm−1 sequentially belong to the bending vibration of NH and the stretching vibration of CN in amide I–III [21]. The above observation indicates that the spore-derived MCDs possess many surface functional groups like double bonds, carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino and alkyl mainly from intrinsic carbohydrates and functional molecules inside the spores. Additionally, weak peaks at 927 cm−1 and 1043 cm−1 can be indexed to the vibration of POH and POC phosphate group [22], probably suggesting the doping of phosphorus atom and the presence of phosphoric groups. These abundant functional groups endow the MCDs with good hydrophilicity and stability in an aqueous system. XPS spectra in Fig. 2F and Fig. S3 further identify the biogenic elements inheriting from the original spore and support the FTIR results. Quantitative results reveal that the MCDs are mainly composed of carbon (58.4%), oxygen (34.2%), nitrogen (6.2%), and phosphorus (1.2%), exhibiting the higher doping of O, N and P than that of previously reported carbon dots [23,24]. These XPS results together with the above FTIR analysis clearly demonstrate that MCDs are not only doped by O, N and P atoms, but also functionalized spontaneously by multiple functional groups like OH, COOH, NH2, and a small amount of other P and N-containing groups. Their presence may improve the electronic properties and surface chemical reactivities of carbon dots, thus imparting MCDs to excellent water solubility and avoiding the requirement of further chemical modification and passivation.

UV–vis spectrum in Fig. 3 A shows one absorption peak together with a shoulder between 200 nm and 300 nm. An intense absorption peak around 235 nm should be attributed to the π-π* electron transition of CC, CN, or N = P in aromatic sp2 domains in the carbon-core of MCDs [25,26]. A shoulder peak at 280 nm might be due to n-π* transition corresponding to CO, COH and CN/CN functional groups on the surface the MCDs [27,28], further demonstrating the presence of surface oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups. The initially obtained MCDs exist in the form of transparent canary brown aqueous solution, but immediately emit a blue color under 360 nm UV light illumination (Fig. 3A inset), showing 400–600 nm emission range with the maxima located at around 440 nm (Fig. 3A). Since such an emission can be visualized directly by naked eyes, these MCDs may be promising candidates for nanosensors or labeling agents. It is noted in Fig. 3B that the emission peaks of MCDs shift to longer wavelengths with decreased intensity after increasing excitation wavelength from 320 to 500 nm by an increment of 20 nm. Such a typical excitation wavelength-dependent characteristic represents the multi-color emission nature of MCDs, potentially serving as multicolor imaging agents. This feature is suggested to be a result of varied fluorescence characteristics of the carbon dots with broad size distribution or the existence of different emissive sites on their surfaces [18,29–34]. The stability studies reveal that the as-obtained MCDs not only show better ionic-strength tolerance in saline solution (0–1 mM NaCl) than conventional QS fluorophore (Fig. S4), but also display good photostability superior to that of Rhodamine B after continuous exposure to UV light (UV lamp of ∼360 nm, 8 W) and 6 months of storage (Fig. S5). Besides, the as-obtained MCDs show negligible fluorescent decay in FBS, DMEM, BHI and agar solution. With the content of FBS increasing (0–83% of volume ratio), the blue and green fluorescent emission is bright and observable, superior to QS fluorophore, as shown in Fig. S6. These excellent fluorescent stabilities would make our MCDs have potential applications for sensing and labeling in physiological saline solution, biological fluids and even in environmental analysis containing salt solution. Nevertheless, pH dependence of fluorescence is observed by naked eye or spectra in Fig. 3C–D when assessing their pH stability, showing great potential as a pH sensor. The fluorescence emission intensity experiences similar changing trend (first increasing then decreasing) under UV light or blue light excitation. The maximum blue emission appears at pH = 4 for UV light excitation while at pH = 6 for blue light excitation. However, the bright emission is also obtained in a certain range by naked eye (Fig. 3C inset). Such pH dependence properties could be attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of surface groups [35], further confirming that the amine and carboxyl groups are present on the surface of MCDs. The gradually negative zeta-potentials in different pH solutions further support this point (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response of MCDs towards Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the capability of the as-obtained MCDs as nanosensors for detecting Fe3+ ions in the surviving environment, the selectivity of MCDs was firstly determined. An acidic buffer solution (pH = 6.0, 10 mM NaAc-HAc) was adopted to take both high emission in weak acidic solution and the common existential situation of heavy metal ions into account. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A–B and Fig. S8, the black bars intuitively represent the fluorescent response of the MCDs toward 38 types of substances under UV light and even blue light irradiation. Fe3+ causes a drastic decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the MCDs, which makes the MCDs as highly selective fluorescent probes for Fe3+ ions. The optical images under UV light observed by naked eye (Fig. 4C–D) reveal that all the mixed solutions show blue color without obvious changing except for MCDs solution with Fe3+ ion (red rectangle). Remarkably, the fluorescence of MCDs solutions containing other substances immediately fades once adding 0.5 mM Fe3+ to respective solution (red bars in Fig. 4A–B and Fig. S8), suggesting the high selectivity to Fe3+ even in the presence of other ions. The observed quenching of MCDs may be due to the fact that Fe3+ coordinates with surface groups of MCDs, disrupting the radiative transition and thus leading to the fluorescent quenching [36]. The further quantitively evaluation in Fig. 4E reveals that the fluorescent emission intensity under the excitation of 360 nm decreases gradually with increasing concentrations of Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 500 μM, which confirms the fluorescence sensitivity of MCDs to Fe3+ ion. The fluorescent quenching efficiency (F0/F-1, F0 and F represent the fluorescence emission intensities of the MCDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively) increases sharply at low concentration and then increases step by step, which is supported by the color change of optical images of MCDs under UV light illumination (Fig. 4F inset). Moreover, the fluorescent quenching efficiency shows a good linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 2.5–100 nM (Fig. 4G). The corresponding fitting equation for linear regression is F0/F–1 = 0.00157[Fe3+] + 0.03388 with a near idea correlation coefficient of 0.995, where [Fe3+] is the concentration of Fe3+ (nM). Thus, the LOD was estimated to be 12.3 nM based on (3S.D.)/k (where S.D. is the standard deviation of the corrected blank signal of the MCDs (n = 7) and k is the slope of the calibration curve). Remarkably, the fluorescent emission of MCDs under blue light excitation also displays similar curve (Fig. 4H). The fluorescent quenching tendency is well fitted to the equation: F0/F–1 = 0.00196[Fe3+] + 0.05031, with the LOD of 15.9 nM. These LODs at different light illumination are relatively low in comparison to the earlier reported values [37–39], as shown in Table S1. The results demonstrate that MCDs could serve as selective and sensitive nanoprobes for the determination of Fe3+ in the complex environmental system. The practical application of MCDs for Fe3+ sensing was investigated in tap water and rain water as well. Good recoveries varying in the range of 98–109% and high analytical precision in Table S2 further verify the feasibility and reliability of MCDs based nanosensors for detecting Fe3+ in practical environments.

Based on the previous reports and as-obtained results, the possible quenching mechanism is proposed. It is reported that carbon dots can be quenched by cations owing to the nonradiative electron-transfer from the carbon dots to cations [40,41]. As we know, the Fe3+ ion with empty orbit has strong binding affinity and fast chelating kinetic with some functional groups on the surface of MCDs through weak interaction, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups, as shown in Fig. 5 A. Such interactions induce the change of the electronic structure or surface state of MCDs, thus affecting the distribution of excitons, finally causing the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through an effective electron transfer process. For further supporting the above proposed mechanism, we performed time-resolved fluorescence decay and UV–vis absorption analysis for the MCDs with different concentration of Fe3+. Fig. 5B shows the fitted luminescence decays of the samples. It is observed that the fluorescence lifetime of MCDs decreases from 5.96 ns to 4.29 ns with the increase of concentration, indicating that the fluorescence quenching is dynamic and involves electron transfer from the excited state of the MCDs to the unfilled orbital of Fe3+, leading to non-radiative electron/hole recombination. The enhanced and broaden UV adsorption bands after the addition of Fe3+ ions in Fig. S9 further suggest that Fe3+ ion with the half-filled 3d orbits, as an acceptor, absorb the electron transferred from the excited state of the MCDs, demonstrating the nonradiative electron/hole recombination annihilation.

To evaluate the further biological applications of MCDs based nanosensors, the cytotoxicity of MCDs against MIHA normal liver cells was conducted using the MTT assays. The cell viabilities are shown to be more than 90% at a concentration below 0.4 mg/mL after 24 h incubation, and over 77% even at a concentration of 0.6 mg/mL (Fig. S10), higher than the minimum permissible limit (70%) by FDA. Such low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility make the MCDs based nanosensors promising candidates in detecting Fe3+ in living cells by cellular imaging. When the MIHA cells were incubated with MCDs for 24 h at 37 °C, three-color fluorescence emissions inside the cells were observed in Fig. 6 top panel under the different wavelength excitation, signifying the potential for multicolor imaging and labeling in vivo due to longer emission. After the addition of exogenous Fe3+ (500 μM) in the growth medium and further incubation for 6 h at 37 °C, the MIHA cells almost emit no obvious fluorescence (Fig. 5 bottom panel). This indicates that the MCDs can be used as nanosensors for intracellular Fe3+ detection. Therefore, spore-derived MCDs not only serve as multicolor imaging agents for cell imaging, but also act as effective probes for the intracellular detection of Fe3+.

Besides, the further biological experiments reveal that the MCDs show certain response to the contamination of E. coli. As illustrated in Fig. S11A, E. coli caused a decaying effect on the fluorescence emission of MCDs compared with B. substilis. The fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with the increase of the concentration of E. coli (Fig. S11B), suggesting the feasibility of E. coli sensing. The fluorescence quenching process follows the regression equation: F/F0 = −0.01935lg[C E.coli ] + 1.0297, showing linearity in the concentration range of 1.75 × 103-1.75 × 108 cfu/mL (Fig. S11C). The LOD was determined to 7.9 × 104 cfu/mL. This indicates the feasibility of detecting E. coli using the linear fitting equation. Such detecting observations could be ascribed to the possible interaction between surface functional groups and FimH protein on the surface of E. coli [42].

In conclusion, fluorescent MCDs with self-functional features were prepared via simple hydrothermal treatment without any following surface passivation. The resultant carbon dots are doped by multiple heteroatoms and show good dispersion, excellent water-solubility, tunable emission and stable fluorescence emission against various ionic strength and biological fluids. The as-prepared MCDs exhibit pH responsive property and highly selective and sensitive detection for Fe3+ ion under different illumination. The resultant MCDs based nanosensors were further successfully applied for monitoring Fe3+ in real tap water and rain water, and even measuring the intracellular Fe3+ through multi-color fluorescence imaging due to broad emission of MCDs. Moreover, the detection of MCDs towards the E. coli bacteria was also demonstrated. These MCDs with low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility may not only serve as a multicolor imaging agent for cell imaging, but also act as an effective probe for Fe3+ and gut bacteria. This work thus provides a promising means to cost-effectively produce versatile and high-quality fluorescent carbon dots as an efficient platform for clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the assistance of XPS characterization from Zhejiang University (ZJU), also thank Ye Bian for the assistance of the product dialysis, and technician Man Hau Yeung for the support of TEM inspection. This work was partially supported by the Early Career Scheme (ECS) with Project 439113, the General Research Fund (GRF) with Project 14209514, 14203715 and 14218516 from the Research Grants Council (RGC) of Hong Kong SAR, and the HKSAR Innovation and Technology Commission (ITC) with ITF Project No. ITS/231/15.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.05.112.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Functional surface engineering of C-Dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging

Facile synthesis of S, N co-doped carbon dots and investigation of their photoluminescence properties

Study of cortex eucommiae by infrared microspectroscopy using synchrotron radiation
pH-sensitive carbon dots for the visualization of regulation of intracellular pH inside living pathogenic fungal Cells
Nitrogen and phosphorus Co-doped carbon nanodots as a novel fluorescent probe for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ in human serum and living cells
Water-soluble and phosphorus-containing carbon dots with strong green fluorescence for cell labeling

Photoluminescent carbon dots directly derived from polyethylene glycol and their application for cellular imaging
Simple and green synthesis of nitrogen-doped photoluminescent carbonaceous nanospheres for bioimaging
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Easy synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from gelatin and their luminescent properties and applications

Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
One-step synthesis of biofunctional carbon quantum dots for bacterial labeling

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yabin Zhang is studying for Ph.D. degree in Prof. Li Zhang’s lab at The Chinese University of Hong Kong. His research interests include the preparation of micro/nanomaterials and their biomedical applications.

Kai Fung Chan received his bachelor’s degree in biomedical engineering from The Chinese University of Hong Kong in 2015. Now he has been a Ph.D. student at Chow Yuk Ho Technology Centre for Innovative Medicine, and Department of Biomedical Engineering at CUHK since 2015, under the supervision of Dr. Li Zhang and Dr. Philip Wai Yan Chiu. His research interests include biomaterial, stem cell therapy, and translational biomedical research.

Ben Wang received his BS from Wuhan Institute of Technology and ME from Hubei University in 2011 and 2014. He is currently a Ph.D. student at The Chinese University of Hong Kong and his research interest is focus on magnetic nanoparticles and their biomedical applications.

Philip Wai Yan Chiu is currently a Professor in the Department of Surgery at The Chinese University of Hong Kong. He graduated from the Faculty of Medicine, Chinese University of Hong Kong in 1994 with two scholarships. He became a fellow of the Royal College of Surgeons of Edinburgh, Hong Kong Academy of Medicine in 2001 and received his Doctor of Medicine at CUHK in 2009. He is also the Director of Chow Yuk Ho Technology Center for Innovative Medicine, and the Director of CUHK Jockey Club Minimal Invasive Surgical Skills Center. His research interests include early diagnosis and treatment of upper gastrointestinal cancers and diseases, minimally invasive and robotic surgery and endoscopic submucosal dissection.

Li Zhang is an Associate Professor in the Department of Mechanical and Automation Engineering, The Chinese University of Hong Kong. He received the Ph.D. from the University of Basel and Paul Scherrer Institute, Switzerland in 2007. And then he worked in the Institute of Robotics and Intelligent Systems (IRIS) at ETH Zurich from 2007 to 21012, before he joined CUHK. His research interests include micro- and nanorobotics for biomedical applications. Dr. Zhang received the Hong Kong RGC Early Career Award in 2013.
